name: Britannia / England
description: The island of Britain south of the Scottish Highlands — modern England and Wales. Core zone of Roman Britain, Anglo-Saxon kingdoms, and the medieval English state.

periods.note: Iron Age Celtic Britain — Brittonic tribes (Catuvellauni, Iceni, Trinovantes, etc.); hillfort culture; Druids; traded with Gaul and the Mediterranean

ethnic_composition.presence: ruling/administrative; Roman towns, villas, and roads; Britannia was one of the most heavily garrisoned provinces
dominant majority (Romanised in south; barely touched in north and Wales)
late-period Saxon shore settlers and foederati

periods.note: Anglo-Saxon Migration and Heptarchy — waves of Angles, Saxons, and Jutes from northwestern Germany displaced or absorbed the Brittonic population; seven kingdoms emerged (Northumbria, Mercia, East Anglia, Wessex, Essex, Kent, Sussex). Viking Great Heathen Army arrived 865; Alfred the Great of Wessex contained them. Conversion to Christianity from 597 (Augustine's mission).

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — Anglo-Saxon settlers replaced Brittonic as ruling class; Old English language established
surviving in Wales, Cornwall, and Cumbria
Danelaw (Yorkshire, East Midlands) from late 9th century

periods.note: Æthelstan unified England 927; briefly lost to Danish King Cnut 1013–1042 (Cnut ruled England, Denmark, and Norway simultaneously). Edward the Confessor restored Wessex line; died childless 1066, triggering the succession crisis.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant (Anglo-Saxon English — Old English language, thegn-based society)
significant in Danelaw regions; assimilating into English

periods.note: Norman Conquest 1066 — William's victory at Hastings replaced the entire Anglo-Saxon aristocracy with Norman French. For 200 years the ruling class spoke French while the peasantry spoke English; by 1300 the languages had merged into Middle English. Plantagenet dynasty (1154) brought more continental entanglement. Magna Carta 1215. Hundred Years' War 1337–1453.

ethnic_composition.presence: ruling class (crown, nobility, church) — French-speaking for two centuries
dominant majority (Anglo-Saxon peasantry, then English-speaking middle class)

periods.note: Tudor England — Henry VII ended the Wars of the Roses; Henry VIII broke with Rome (1534) and created the Church of England; Elizabeth I presided over the Elizabethan golden age (Shakespeare, Drake, Raleigh). Spanish Armada defeated 1588.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant — post-Norman composite English identity fully consolidated

periods.note: Stuart era — James I united English and Scottish crowns in personal union; Civil War 1642–1651 (Charles I executed); Cromwellian Commonwealth; Restoration 1660; Glorious Revolution 1688 established constitutional monarchy; culminating in the Act of Union with Scotland 1707.

ethnic_composition.presence: Irish minority, subject people under English plantation policy
Scottish Highlanders and Irish — subordinate; Highland Clearances begin
Irish (Great Famine 1845–52 halved the Irish population); Scottish
